title: Slugify a title
summary: Turn a messy title into a clean URL slug.
why: String cleanup shows up everywhere in real Python work.
brief: Write a function slugify(title) that lowercases the text, trims outer whitespace, replaces spaces/underscores with hyphens, removes non alphanumeric characters except hyphens, and collapses repeated hyphens.
starter_code:
import re

def slugify(title: str) -> str:
    # your code here
    raise NotImplementedError
test_code:
assert slugify(' Hello Python World ') == 'hello-python-world'
assert slugify('API__for Beginners!!!') == 'api-for-beginners'
assert slugify('Already---clean') == 'already-clean'
hints: Use the re module to remove characters you do not want.
Collapse repeated spaces or hyphens at the end.

title: Filter paid invoices
summary: Find paid invoices and total their value.
why: A huge amount of scripting is filtering records and summing values.
brief: Write a function paid_total(invoices) that returns the total amount for invoices where status == 'paid'. Round to 2 decimal places.
hints: Loop through the invoices or use sum() with a generator.
Only add amounts when status is 'paid'.
Use round(total, 2) once at the end.

title: Normalize API payloads
summary: Clean incoming JSON-like data into a reliable shape.
why: Real systems spend a lot of time taming messy inputs.
brief: Write normalize_user(payload) that returns a dict with keys name, email, is_active. Name should be title-cased and stripped. Email should be lowercase and stripped. Missing is_active should default to False.
test_code:
user = normalize_user({'name': '  charlie b ', 'email': ' TEST@EXAMPLE.COM  '})
assert user == {'name': 'Charlie B', 'email': 'test@example.com', 'is_active': False}
user2 = normalize_user({'name': 'sam', 'email': 'sam@example.com', 'is_active': 1})
assert user2['is_active'] is True
hints: strip(), lower(), and title() do most of the cleanup.
bool(...) can normalize the active flag.

title: Build a tiny bank account class
summary: Create a small class with state and safety checks.
why: Classes make sense faster when the state is concrete.
brief: Implement a BankAccount class with methods deposit(amount), withdraw(amount), and balance property. Reject zero or negative deposits and reject withdrawals that exceed balance by raising ValueError.
test_code:
acct = BankAccount()
acct.deposit(50)
acct.withdraw(20)
assert acct.balance == 30
try:
    acct.deposit(0)
    raise AssertionError('expected ValueError')
except ValueError:
    pass
try:
    acct.withdraw(100)
    raise AssertionError('expected ValueError')
except ValueError:
    pass
hints: Store the running balance on self.
Use raise ValueError('message') for invalid actions.
A @property lets balance behave like a read-only attribute.
